serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 80 g, representing a small side dish of diced and grated daikon dressed with ponzu sauce as served in Japanese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Daikon radish, raw (NDB No. 11206) and Ponzu sauce, estimated from USDA SR Legacy and USDA FoodData Central branded ponzu entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for diced-and-grated daikon with ponzu sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw daikon radish (approximately 85–90% of dish weight) with USDA FoodData Central data for ponzu sauce (approximately 10–15% of dish weight). The composite is weighted at approximately 90 g daikon per 100 g dish and 10 g ponzu per 100 g dish. Daikon per 100 g: energy 18 kcal, protein 0.6 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 4.1 g, fibre 1.6 g, sodium 21 mg, sugar 2.5 g. Ponzu sauce per 100 g: energy 52 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 9.5 g, sodium 3600 mg. Composite per 100 g dish estimated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — daikon radish and low-carbohydrate vegetable analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for diced-and-grated daikon with ponzu sauce. GI is estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of raw daikon radish and similar non-starchy root vegetables as low-GI foods (GI range 10–20). Ponzu sauce contributes minimal glycemic load due to its low carbohydrate content per serving. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 3.76) / 100 ≈ 0.6.

hypertension.guidance: Ponzu sauce contributes significant sodium (approximately 379 mg per 100 g of the dish); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium ponzu where available, as regular consumption of soy-based dressings can meaningfully contribute to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diced and grated daikon with ponzu sauce has a very low glycemic index (estimated 15) and low carbohydrate content (~4.7 g per 100 g), making it a suitable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; the dietary fibre from daikon further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat, low calorie, high water content with moderate sodium caveat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is very low in total fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and calories (22 kcal per 100 g), and daikon contains isothiocyanates with potential anti-inflammatory properties; however, the sodium contribution from ponzu sauce warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, and low-sodium ponzu alternatives are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Diced and Grated Daikon with Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese side dish consisting of Japanese white radish (daikon) that is both diced into small cubes and grated into a fine, moist texture, then dressed with ponzu sauce — a tangy, citrus-infused soy-based condiment made from soy sauce, yuzu or other citrus juice, mirin, and dashi. The dish delivers a refreshing, light, and slightly tart flavour profile with a crisp and juicy texture from the raw daikon. It is commonly served as a palate-cleansing accompaniment to grilled meats, gyudon (beef rice bowls), or other rich Japanese dishes. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (approximately 22 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides modest dietary fibre (1.4 g per 100 g) and vitamin C (17 mg per 100 g). The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the ponzu sauce (approximately 379 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Daikon is a staple ingredient in Japanese cuisine and is valued for its digestive enzyme content (diastase) and mild diuretic properties. This dish is suitable for vegetarians only if the ponzu sauce is made without fish-based dashi; standard commercial ponzu typically contains fish-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish (diced and grated daikon with ponzu sauce). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw daikon radish with USDA FoodData Central data for ponzu sauce in approximate proportions. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database data for raw daikon and similar non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy (raw daikon radish) and USDA FoodData Central (ponzu sauce), weighted approximately 90:10 by mass. No single approved-source entry for this exact dish was found. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae), B-vitamins (vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg), folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported or reliably derivable for this composite dish from the available approved sources. 3. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are 0.0 as daikon is a non-starchy vegetable with negligible fat content and ponzu sauce contains no meaningful fat; these are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FoodData Central source data. 4. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with raw daikon and similar non-starchy root vegetables per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. Allergen note: standard ponzu sauce contains soy and fish-derived dashi; this dish is not suitable for fish-allergic individuals or strict vegans unless a vegan ponzu variant is used. 6. The food_group assignment of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' is preserved from the input record as this dish functions primarily as a condiment side in the context of Japanese restaurant service, though it could also be classified under Vegetables. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is used as closest parent (daikon food entity); no exact FoodOn entry for this composite dish exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest parent for radishes and similar root vegetables.
